variant_description: Pure M/M/1 canonical variant — the simplest possible M/M/1 simulation. Infinite buffer, FCFS discipline, stationary analysis, no balking/reneging. Approximately 30 lines of Python following the SimPy Bank tutorial pattern (context row 3). Serves Scenarios 1, 2, and 4 directly and Scenario 3 as a baseline for variability comparisons. SimPy is the target technology because the Bank tutorial provides a reference implementation, but this variant can be implemented in essentially any DES tool (arena, anylogic, simio, jaamsim, salabim, ciw, etc.).

M/M/1/K finite-buffer variant (also called M/M/1/K or M/M/1/N). Relaxes the canonical model's infinite-buffer assumption: the queue has a maximum capacity K, and customers arriving when the queue is full are blocked/lost. Introduces a new metric (loss rate or blocking probability) and is the model of choice for bounded-capacity systems like telephone switchboards or manufacturing buffers. Target technology not yet committed.

M/M/1 with reneging variant. Relaxes the canonical model's 'no reneging' assumption: customers who have joined the queue leave before being served if their wait exceeds a patience threshold (typically itself exponentially distributed). Serves customer-impatience scenarios in service operations research. Target technology not yet committed.

M/M/1 with balking variant. Relaxes the canonical model's 'no balking' assumption: arriving customers observe the queue state and decide whether to join based on the observed queue length or their patience. Serves drive-thru, retail, and service scenarios where the decision to enter a queue is itself modeled. Target technology not yet committed.
